_doc: Prediction ledger for the After Hours / Before the Bell letters. Every falsifiable call a letter makes gets one entry when made. Each close-of-market run MUST: (1) resolve any prediction whose horizon passed or whose falsifier/confirmer triggered, (2) write resolution_note explaining WHY it was right or wrong (mechanism, not just outcome), (3) distill a lesson into playbook.md when the resolution teaches something, (4) update the calibration tally. Verdicts: right | wrong | partial | timing (structurally right, timing off) | expired (unresolvable). Max 3 new predictions per issue; a call without a falsifier doesn't get logged, it gets rewritten until it has one.

calibration.notes: Updated 2026-06-15: 4 resolved — 2 right (1 high, 1 medium), 1 partial (medium), 1 wrong (medium). By tier: high 1/1 clean; medium now 1/3 clean (oil-is-the-tell RIGHT, gamma-reclaim partial, CPI wrong). The medium tier earned its first clean win, and it was a MECHANISM call (equities follow crude, not headlines) graded across adversarial tests — consistent with the early read that our mechanism reads are the strength. The two medium misses both failed on level-quoting discipline, not mechanism (lesson 7). Watch whether medium keeps converting now that level-keyed claims name their governing standard.

predictions.claim: Supply-side AI (NVDA, AVGO, MRVL, TSM, MU, VRT, DELL equal-weight) outperforms demand-side platforms (GOOGL, META, AAPL, MSFT equal-weight) from 2026-06-01 baseline.
predictions.falsifier: Demand-side basket leads by >5pts at quarterly review, or two consecutive supply-side bellwether guides cut (AVGO June 3 was strike one).

predictions.claim: GOOGL is a buy at $380.34; Berkshire's $350 entry acts as an effective floor and the raise overhang clears.
predictions.falsifier: Sustained closes below $350 (the market rejecting Buffett's price) or underwriters pricing the public tranche materially below $350.

predictions.claim: The market gives us at least one 5-10% S&P pullback within a year of inception; 25% cash reserve gets deployed into it.
predictions.falsifier: S&P melts up for 12 months with no 5% drawdown; cash drag was pure cost.

predictions.claim: Market looks through a hot headline May CPI (~4.2% y/y, energy-driven) IF core prints 0.3% m/m or below; equities end CPI day flat-to-up. A core print above 0.3% breaks the tape (>1% down day) by putting a June 17 hike in play.
predictions.falsifier: Core <=0.3% and S&P still falls >1.5% (resilience thesis wrong), or core >0.3% and market rallies anyway (hike fear overstated).
predictions.resolution_note: Falsifier fired verbatim: core printed 0.2% m/m (below our 0.3% line) and the S&P still fell 1.62%, closing at the lows. Mechanism autopsy: the call modeled CPI as the day's ONLY variable. Three things beat the soft core: (1) the war escalated overnight (IRGC strikes on US bases in three countries, Trump threatening to attack 'very hard') — the call's conditional never accounted for the other live binary; (2) composition — supercore accelerated to +0.55% m/m, worst since March 2024 and accelerating since fall 2025, so the 'soft' core was goods deflation masking services heat (the reader-desk flag at 8:45 was right); (3) a new flow story (the $3.6T OpenAI/Anthropic/SpaceX IPO pipeline) gave tech sellers a third reason. Right-for-the-right-reason check, inverted: HALF the mechanism worked — rate traders did look through the headline (10Y eased to 4.52%, June hike bets pared), exactly as we argued. The equity half failed because equities had problems the bond market doesn't price. Lesson distilled to playbook: on multi-binary days, condition calls on levels, not on a single catalyst.

predictions.claim: The market is trading the Iran deal, not the strikes: WTI stays below $95 and equities keep absorbing Iran headlines (no >2% down day attributable to Iran) unless the Strait of Hormuz actually closes.
predictions.falsifier: WTI closes above $95 without Hormuz closure, or an Iran headline alone produces a >2% S&P down day that doesn't recover within two sessions.
predictions.resolution_note: Resolved one session ahead of the June 16 horizon because the confirmer triggered decisively: the deal was declared 'complete' Sunday, WTI cracked to $80.75 (-4.8%, an 8-week low) and the S&P rallied +1.65% to 7,554.29. Neither falsifier ever fired across the full window: WTI never closed above $95 (it fell the whole period, from ~$90 on June 9 to $80.75), and no Iran headline alone produced an unrecovered >2% S&P down day. Mechanism check — right for the right reason: the entire thesis was that equities follow CRUDE, not the headline TEXT, and the window delivered the cleanest possible proof — June 11 Iran DECLARED Hormuz closed and oil FELL 4% while stocks rallied 1.75%; June 12 Tehran disputed the draft text and oil fell 3% while stocks rose; today the signing itself is what's priced and oil/equities moved in lockstep with the de-escalation. Four consecutive sessions, including two where the bearish headline was as loud as it gets, all graded by crude. The hypothesis ('oil is the tell on war headlines') is hereby promoted to a playbook Active lesson — it has now been right across multiple independent tests including adversarial ones. Caveat banked for honesty: the regime that made oil the clean tell was a market that WANTED the deal; in a tape already fearful for other reasons, a crude move might not dominate sentiment as cleanly — the lesson is 'when oil and the headline disagree, trust oil,' not 'oil predicts everything.'

predictions.claim: The mechanical index-fund bid into June 22 S&P inclusion limits MRVL downside: stock holds above $250 through inclusion despite giving back 7.6% today.
predictions.falsifier: MRVL closes below $250 before June 22 (inclusion bids don't defend prices in a tape this twitchy).

predictions.claim: Conditional on a tame core CPI (<=0.3% m/m June 10): SPX closes back above ~7,440 (the level where dealer hedging flips from amplifying moves to dampening them) within two sessions, i.e. by the June 12 close, setting up a calmer pinned tape into June 19 triple-witching.
predictions.falsifier: Core <=0.3% and SPX still fails to close above 7,440 by June 12 (dip-buyers weaker than we think, or the gamma framework is decoration). If core prints >0.3% the condition is void - mark expired, not wrong.
predictions.resolution_note: Graded at the deadline: SPX closed 7,431.46 — ABOVE the dealer-hedging flip the call's own parenthetical defined as the level (7,424 on both feeds at the June 11 recompute; first close above the flip since June 4), but BELOW the literal 7,440 the call quoted, which was the June 9 ESTIMATE of where that flip sat. Mechanism check, which is what the claim was actually about: tame core (0.2%) → dip-buyers reclaimed the level where dealers stop amplifying, in two sessions (+165 pts off the June 10 close), with the predicted calmer pinned tape observably arriving (VIX 19.01, near-dated dealer gamma positive on both feeds June 11, range compressed to ~0.7% from 2.14%). The number check fails: 7,431.46 < 7,440, and the morning brief pre-committed 'no partial credit for 7,439' — a sentence written hours AFTER our own recompute had moved the flip to 7,424, which manufactured tonight's ambiguity. Verdict partial, harsh in both directions: not 'right' because the printed line didn't print and we said it had to; not 'wrong' because the level the call defined itself by (the flip) was reclaimed and the regime change it predicted is in place. Lesson distilled to playbook: when a call keys to a moving computed level, the claim must state at logging time whether the number or the recomputed level governs the grade — and never re-affirm a stale number in print when a fresher compute is already in hand.

predictions.claim: June 17 FOMC holds rates (no hike) — the soft core m/m plus a war-driven headline gives cover — but the supercore acceleration keeps the tilt hawkish: December-hike odds in futures stay above 50% through the meeting.
predictions.falsifier: An actual hike on June 17 (hold half wrong), or post-meeting December-hike odds collapsing below 30% (hawkish-tilt half wrong).

predictions.claim: The amplified tape persists: until SPX closes back above the 7,420-7,450 zone where dealer hedging flips, daily swings stay wide — at least one of the next three sessions (June 11, 12, 15) prints a 1.5%+ high-to-low intraday range.
predictions.falsifier: All three sessions print high-low ranges under 1% while SPX remains below 7,420 (the gamma framework says that combination shouldn't happen; if it does, the framework is decoration).
predictions.resolution_note: Confirmed on the first of three eligible sessions: June 11 printed a 7,257.33-7,412.68 high-to-low range — 2.14%, well past the 1.5% bar — while SPX stayed below the 7,420-7,450 flip zone the entire session (high 7,412.68). Right-for-the-right-reason check: the mechanism was amplification-in-both-directions, and that is exactly what printed — a hot-PPI flush through the 7,265 put wall in the morning, then a 155-point rip on the strike-cancellation headlines in the afternoon, both legs in ONE session below the flip. The direction-agnostic framing (written the night before, when the temptation was to make it a bearish call) is what earned the grade; an equivalent 'wide range because more selling' claim would have been right for the wrong reason. Note for honesty: the catalyst (a near-end to the war) was not predicted — only the tape's amplification of whatever arrived, which is all the call claimed.

predictions.claim: The software de-rate is multiple compression with momentum, not a one-day CFO scare: ADBE does not reclaim its pre-print level — every close stays below $233.38 (the June 10 close) through June 19 — despite the beat-and-raise and the tripled AI ARR.
predictions.falsifier: Any ADBE close above $233.38 on or before June 19 (the market re-reads the print as the AI-monetization evidence it actually contained, and the de-rate was positioning, not regime).

predictions.claim: The floor moved up for good this time: with dealer positioning nearly neutral, oil sub-$90, and the deal in signing range, SPX does not CLOSE below 7,265 (the June 10 put wall / June 11 intraday hold) on any session before the June 17 FOMC decision.
predictions.falsifier: Any SPX close below 7,265 before the June 17 FOMC statement (deal-slip or Fed-fear selling overwhelms the improved positioning floor; per the playbook, the call is keyed to the LEVEL, whatever the headline that breaks it).

predictions.claim: The shock absorbers are back and they hold: every session from June 15 through June 19 triple-witching that BEGINS above the dealer-hedging flip (prior close above ~7,420) prints a high-to-low intraday range under 1.5% — dealers long gamma lean against moves, including on FOMC day. Grading governs by the recomputed flip each night, not the 7,420 number (lesson 7 applied at logging time).
predictions.falsifier: Any session that starts above the flip and prints a 1.5%+ high-to-low range (positive-gamma dampening failed when it mattered). Sessions that START below the flip don't count toward either side — if a weekend deal-slip gaps the tape below the flip and keeps it there, mark expired, not wrong.

predictions.claim: The SpaceX debut was real demand, not a flip: SPCX does not close below its $135 offer price on any session through June 30. Mechanism: the fixed-price structure meant no underwriter-engineered pop to fade — the $160.95 close is the market's own bid, and breaking the offer price ('breaking issue') is the specific failure that would re-arm the $3.6T IPO-pipeline fear for the September listings.
predictions.falsifier: Any SPCX close below $135 on or before June 30 (the market resents the supply after all; the pipeline-absorption read from day one was a peace-rally artifact).

predictions.claim: The same positive-gamma machinery that pinned the tape and capped panic also caps the JOY: SPX does not CLOSE above 7,600 (the June 1 record, 7,599.96) before the June 19 triple-witching close. Mechanism: with net GEX at a cycle-high positive (+$80B/+$101B), dealers are long gamma and SELL into the 7,555 call wall, so clearing a fresh all-time high requires the wall to migrate up first — which takes either time or a new catalyst the calendar (BoJ/FOMC, both more likely to inject caution than fuel) doesn't obviously provide. Grading governs by the 7,600 record level (a round, fixed, widely-watched number), not by the nightly-recomputed wall (lesson 7 applied at logging time).
predictions.falsifier: Any SPX close above 7,600 on or before June 19 (the wall migrated up faster than the positive-gamma cap predicts, or a fresh catalyst overwhelmed it — the framework is decoration on the upside).

predictions.claim: The BoJ's near-certain 25bp hike to 1.0% (80-97% priced) is a sell-the-fact non-event for US tech, NOT the long-feared yen-carry-unwind trigger: no >2% Nasdaq down day attributable to a yen spike in the two sessions following the June 16-17 decision. Mechanism: a fully-priced hike with Gov. Ueda absent is most likely accompanied by dovish-leaning forward guidance to avoid a disorderly yen move; the carry unwind needs a SURPRISE (hawkish dissent or an unexpectedly steep path), not the base case.
predictions.falsifier: Nasdaq prints a >2% down day within two sessions of the BoJ decision that coheres with a sharp yen appreciation (USD/JPY breaking down hard) — i.e. the carry-unwind tail the wiki has flagged as the #1 macro risk actually fires. (A >2% tech down day driven by the FOMC or a deal slip, with the yen quiet, does NOT falsify this — the claim is specifically about the BoJ/yen channel.)
